nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing rocky rapids and boulder-strewn stretches of blackwater and clearwater rivers. Juveniles inhabit shallow rapid zones beneath flat rocks, while adults occupy larger cavities among and beneath submerged boulders.

decorPreferences: large smooth boulders and rocky caves replicating Xingu rapids
smooth flat rocks for aufwuchs grazing
ceramic or slate breeding caves with snug opening
driftwood for additional biofilm and territory definition
minimal fine substrate — sand or bare-bottom acceptable
strong directional flow (4–5× tank volume per hour)
high oxygenation via surface agitation or airstones

diet.notes: The Gold Nugget Pleco is a herbivore-leaning omnivore whose primary diet in the wild consists of algae — including diatoms and filamentous types such as Spirogyra — and biofilm grazed from exposed rock surfaces. In the aquarium, the diet should be built around high-quality sinking algae wafers, spirulina-based pellets, and blanched vegetables such as zucchini, sweet potato, and cucumber. Allowing rock and glass surfaces to develop a natural coating of aufwuchs (the biofilm community of algae, bacteria, and microorganisms that colonises submerged surfaces) benefits long-term health. Occasional protein supplementation — frozen bloodworm, brine shrimp, or high-protein gel food — is useful for growth and conditioning. Overfeeding should be avoided, as uneaten food degrades water quality rapidly. Because the species is primarily nocturnal, feeding after lights-out is generally recommended.

goodWith: medium-to-large characids (e.g., silver dollars, larger tetras)
peaceful mid-water cichlids of similar size
avoidWith: other Gold Nugget Plecos (intense conspecific territorial aggression in males)
small fish under 5 cm (risk of being harassed or crushed against substrate)
aggressive cichlids that compete for cave territories
species requiring cool or soft-water conditions incompatible with warm Xingu parameters

imageCandidates.notes: Anne Blindheim; 2012x1224. Tack-sharp aquarium adult showing the diagnostic jet-black body dense with bold yellow spots and vivid yellow-edged dorsal and caudal fins — the definitive Gold Nugget look, whole fish framed.
Tylwyth Eldar; 6000x4000. Full lateral view of a dark, yellow-spotted adult on driftwood in an aquarium with a clearly yellow-margined caudal fin; good in-tank context shot.
Fabio Olmos; 1575x1050. Sharp in-hand wild specimen from the Xingu showing the full body profile with bright yellow-tipped dorsal and caudal fins and yellow spotting; adds wild-collection variety.

summary: The Gold Nugget Pleco (Baryancistrus xanthellus) is a striking armored catfish from the boulder-strewn rapids of the Rio Xingu in Brazil, recognizable by its dark body covered in bold yellow spots and yellow-edged fins. Adults reach 25–30 cm and tend to be strongly territorial toward their own kind, making them intermediate-level fish to keep.
careNotes:
The Gold Nugget Pleco (Baryancistrus xanthellus) originates from the fast-flowing, highly oxygenated boulder rapids of the Rio Xingu and Rio Iriri in Pará state, Brazil — a habitat threatened by the Belo Monte hydroelectric dam. This background dictates nearly all husbandry requirements: the species demands warm water (27–30°C), excellent oxygenation, strong directional flow (4–5× tank volume per hour), and pristine water quality with consistently low nitrate and nitrite levels. A minimum 380 L tank with a footprint of at least 150 cm is the widely accepted baseline for a single adult kept long-term; larger setups tend to improve health outcomes and reduce disease pressure.

The Gold Nugget Pleco is sold in the trade under several L-numbers — the lettered codes used to identify loricariid catfish (plecos) not yet formally described or widely known by scientific name at the time of their introduction. L18, L85, and LDA060 are all accepted as Baryancistrus xanthellus. L81, sometimes called the stardust form because of its finer spot pattern, originates from São Félix do Xingu upstream of confirmed B. xanthellus localities and is provisionally treated as the same species, though its taxonomic status remains unresolved — it is often sold as a distinct variant. L177, the large-spot gold nugget from the lower Xingu, is also assigned to B. xanthellus. All of these trade forms require identical husbandry.

Males tend to become strongly territorial toward conspecifics once sexually mature, which occurs at around 3–4 years of age, and are generally not recommended to be kept together unless the aquarium is very large and provides multiple well-separated cave territories. Juveniles are comparatively peaceful. The species is well suited to community tanks with robust mid-water fish that do not compete for bottom territory.

Biofilm and algae grazing are central to long-term health; allowing rock surfaces to colonise with aufwuchs and providing sinking vegetable-based wafers, blanched vegetables, and occasional frozen protein gives the fish a diet close to what it encounters in the wild. The species is sensitive to copper-based medications, so any fish treatment containing copper should be avoided in a tank where Gold Nugget Plecos are kept. The species cannot tolerate organic waste accumulation — weekly 25–30% water changes and robust mechanical and biological filtration are considered essential. Its sensitivity and cost make this an intermediate-level species despite not requiring a specialist setup.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is extremely rare and is widely regarded as one of the most challenging feats in the pleco hobby. The Gold Nugget Pleco is a cave spawner: males select and defend a snug cave (a ceramic tube or slate cave with an opening of around 6–7 cm is commonly used), court females, and guard the adhesive egg clutch until hatching, which takes approximately 7–10 days. The male fans and guards the fry through yolk-sac absorption. Success requires a very large, mature aquarium of at least 400 L, pristine water, strong flow that replicates the Xingu rapids, superior conditioning of both sexes over months on a varied diet, and appropriate cave structures. Fish generally need to reach 3–4 years of age and around 12 cm or more before breeding condition is achieved. Fry grow slowly, at approximately 1 cm per two months, and are sensitive to nitrate — grow-out water is generally kept below 10 ppm. Only a handful of hobbyist successes have been documented worldwide.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Baryancistrus xanthellus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided temperature 27–32°C, pH 6.4–7.6, hardness 2–14°H, minimum tank base 120×45 cm, diet (aufwuchs/algae with protein supplement), conspecific aggression profile, L-number assignments (L018, L085, L177, LDA060), native range (Volta Grande do Xingu and Rio Iriri), and breeding difficulty notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Baryancistrus xanthellus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (29.7 cm TL, 510 g), taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes, Hypostominae), native range (Volta Grande do Xingu above Belo Monte falls and Rio Iriri), habitat (shallow rapid stretches under rocks for young; large boulder cavities for adults), diet (algae, bryozoans, chironomid larvae). IUCN Near Threatened status noted.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Gold Nugget Plecos: Treasure from the Xingu
additional.notes: Confirmed warm-water requirement (~86°F/30°C), high flow and oxygenation demands, Rio Xingu native range (Belo Monte to above São Félix do Xingu including Iriri), diet supplementation (zucchini, sweet potato, green-based foods), adult size ~23 cm reported in article context, L-number equivalences L018/L081/L085/L177/LDA060.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Baryancistrus sp. cf. xanthellus (L081)
additional.notes: Cross-reference for L081 variant. Confirmed temperature 27–32°C, pH 6.4–7.6, hardness 54–268 ppm, adult size 150–180 mm SL, tank base 120×45 cm minimum. L081 ("stardust") originates from São Félix do Xingu, upstream of confirmed B. xanthellus localities; provisionally same species but taxonomic status unresolved. Captive breeding noted as extremely rare.

additional.name: Aquariadise — Gold Nugget Pleco Care Guide
additional.notes: Supplementary trade source. Confirmed temperature 77–84°F (25–29°C), pH 6.5–7.5, gH 2–15, tank minimum 120 gallons (454 L) for long-term; used 380 L as conservative consensus. Lifespan ~10 years cited; 8–15 range adopted to reflect expert-kept longevity reports. Copper sensitivity confirmed. Nocturnal feeding schedule. Growth rate ~1 cm/2 months.

fish.breedingNotes: The Gold Nugget Pleco is a cave spawner in which the male guards the adhesive egg clutch and subsequent fry. Captive breeding has been documented only a handful of times globally. Achieving it requires a mature aquarium of 400 L or more, strong flow, pristine water quality, fish that have reached sexual maturity at around 3–4 years of age, and prolonged conditioning on a varied diet. Fry grow slowly, at approximately 1 cm per two months.
